executive_summary.opening_paragraph: This document is the output of our discovery process. Over the past 60 days we have gathered financial, legal, tax, and operational data on you and the business, met with you both, reviewed three years of returns and statements, and analyzed the operating agreement, ownership structure, insurance program, and the 2014 will set. What follows is our recommended path forward — both the immediate priorities and the longer-term strategy that protects what you have built at HIS and accelerates what you are building toward a transaction in the next three to five years.
executive_summary.two_themes_paragraph: Two themes shape this plan. First, HIS is approaching a value range — preliminarily $32M to $48M against trailing adjusted EBITDA — where the cost of inaction on entity, estate, and tax structure compounds rapidly. The Kennesaw facility sitting inside the operating LLC, the 2014 wills, the unfunded buy/sell with Derek, and a $2M umbrella behind a $25M-plus net worth are not gaps that get cheaper with time. Second, you have unsolicited inbound interest from both a strategic regional MEP roll-up and a PE financial buyer — which means the planning we do in the next 12 months determines how much of the eventual sale value you keep, how cleanly Derek is taken care of, and whether the structure is ready when the right buyer arrives rather than reactive to one.
executive_summary.what_this_means_closer: Acted on together, the five priorities above close the largest unfunded exposures on your balance sheet — a $25M-plus liability gap, the unfunded buy/sell with Derek, no ILIT-owned estate liquidity, no key-person coverage, and no receiving trusts for future estate transfers. Layered with the entity restructuring, PTET election, cost segregation, and GRAT/IDGT/SLAT planning detailed in Sections 1 through 7, the combined estate-tax savings run into the eight figures (children's trusts $4M–$8M, GRAT remainder ~$4.2M, IDGT transfer $8M–$15M, SLAT $5.4M–$16.2M, GST allocation $50M+ over the long horizon), with $250K-plus of recurring annual income-tax and yield benefit on top. The combined impact funds professional fees many times over and — more importantly — positions HIS for a transaction in three to five years from a far stronger structural starting point than the one we found at engagement.

our_process.intro_paragraph: Financial planning is not a one-time event — it is the ongoing infrastructure that connects your business decisions, tax posture, estate documents, insurance program, and personal balance sheet so they move together rather than in isolation. This plan is the second milestone in a four-stage relationship.

stages.name: Discovery (completed)
stages.body: Over the past 60 days we have collected and reviewed three years of business and personal returns, the HIS operating agreement, the 2014 wills, current insurance summaries, the personal balance sheet, and mortgage statements. We met with both of you, reviewed Marcus's 88% / Derek's 12% ownership and the personal guarantee on the Truist revolver, and pressure-tested the 3-5 year transaction posture against the two unsolicited approaches that came in over the past 14 months.

stages.name: Plan delivery (today)
stages.body: This document is the deliverable. It carries the strategic frame, the prioritized recommendations across business and personal lenses, the implementation roadmap, the decisions we need from you, and the partner roster — including the Estate Attorney and CPA candidates we are introducing to fill the current gaps.

stages.name: Implementation
stages.body: The 12-18 months following delivery is where the plan is executed. PSA quarterbacks the partners — Estate Attorney, M&A Counsel, CPA, Insurance Broker, Banker, Valuation Provider — against the sequenced roadmap. Most of the high-impact items (umbrella, ILIT, buy/sell, entity separation, PTET, recap) land within the first 120 days; the GRAT/IDGT/SLAT and pre-transaction work runs through year two.

stages.name: Ongoing review
stages.body: Once the structure is in place, we move to the cadence laid out in Section 13: quarterly tax check-ins, semiannual investment reviews, annual plan reviews, and triggered reviews around any material change (transaction LOI, valuation update, family event, regulatory shift). The plan is meant to be a living document, not a binder on a shelf.

our_process.how_to_read_paragraph: The Executive Summary is your 90-second readout. The Findings & Observations section explains where you stand today — what is working and where the opportunities are. The Recommendations sections are our specific guidance — Business first (Sections 1 through 7), Personal second (Sections 8 through 12). The Implementation Roadmap is your project plan. The Decisions Needed page is what we will discuss at our next meeting. The Glossary at the back defines every technical term used inline.

findings_observations.intro_paragraph: Based on what we have reviewed, here is where you stand today — what is working, and where we see opportunities to strengthen your position before the transaction window opens.

strengths.body: Profitable, fast-growing business with a defensible niche. HIS has compounded revenue from $28.4M in 2022 to $42.8M in 2024 (22.7% three-year CAGR) with EBITDA margin expanding from 13.0% to 15.0% — in a vertical (data center MEP at 55% of mix) where demand is structurally accelerating, not cyclical. Backlog of $48M at Q1 2026 represents roughly 14 months of forward revenue.

Strong operating partner. Derek Chen owns 12% (vested through the 2014 profits-interest plan) and is genuinely capable of running the business. That is what gives a future transaction its optionality — most owner-led specialty mechanical contractors do not have a credible #2, and buyers price that gap aggressively.

Clean S-Corp election and three years of clean returns. The S-election was filed at formation and held without interruption; basis tracking and K-1 issuance are orderly, which materially simplifies the F-reorganization sequence in Section 1.

Excellent insurability for both Marcus and Catherine. Marcus is a non-smoker 10+ years at preferred rates; Catherine has never smoked and is in excellent health. This matters — the ILIT survivorship layer, the buy/sell on Derek, the individual DI, and the hybrid LTC all get materially better pricing while you are this side of 55.

Engaged ownership willing to invest in long-term planning. Marcus and Catherine came to this engagement asking the right questions about Derek, the children's age-35 restriction, Eleanor's future care, and Catherine's post-exit real estate vision. That posture is itself the single biggest predictor of whether a plan like this gets executed.
Existing 401(k) and IRA infrastructure in place. We are optimizing — adding a profit-sharing layer, a cash-balance plan, and Roth catch-up — not starting from scratch.

bullets: The Kennesaw headquarters and fabrication facility (~$4.2M) is held inside the operating LLC. This exposes the property to operating-business liability and forfeits multiple tax-planning opportunities — cost segregation, §469 grouping, separate appreciation tracking, and clean stock-vs-asset transaction optionality.

No holding-company layer above the operating entity. This limits flexibility for pre-transaction estate freezes, charitable gifting of operating-company stock, basis planning, and the GRAT/IDGT/SLAT moves that depend on a non-voting class.

Voting and non-voting interests have not been recapitalized. Without the recap, gifted or sold business value carries no defensible LOM/minority discount — leaving roughly $7.4M of valuation discount on the table on Marcus's $24.75M non-voting share.

Operating agreement is generic and does not contemplate transfer restrictions, buy/sell mechanics, or Derek's role through a transition. Buyers routinely amend at the table at a $25K-$100K cost — and worse, on terms negotiated under transaction pressure.

Wills date from 2014, when net worth was approximately $3M. They do not name a guardian for Sophie (born after the will set), do not contemplate the current scale, and pre-date every trust structure we now recommend.
No revocable trust in place — the entire ~$5M of titled probate assets would pass through Georgia probate, exposing roughly $50K-$150K of avoidable attorney fees plus 9-18 months of court timeline.

No DPOA, healthcare directive, or HIPAA authorization for either spouse, and no adult-child HIPAA/healthcare POA for Olivia (now 19, at UGA). A medical event in the next 12 months without these documents triggers a Georgia conservatorship proceeding ($5K-$25K and weeks of delay).

Projected federal estate tax exposure of approximately $11.4M – $17.6M depending on valuation, with no current mitigation strategies — no ILIT, no annual exclusion gifting program, no children's trusts, no GRAT/IDGT/SLAT structure, no GST allocation.

Personal umbrella sits at $2M against a household net worth materially north of $25M, with auto liability at $250K/$250K (sub-umbrella attachment). This is the largest mismatched exposure on the personal balance sheet and the cheapest gap to close.

No buy/sell life insurance funding. If Marcus or Derek dies tomorrow, the surviving owner has neither the cash nor the contractual mechanism to execute a buyout, and Marcus's personal guarantee on the $4M Truist revolver becomes Catherine's problem overnight.
No ILIT-owned life insurance for estate liquidity. Marcus's $2M term policy expires in 2031 and is owned personally — meaning the death benefit is fully includible in his taxable estate.
No key-person coverage on Derek and no business overhead expense (BOE) coverage on Marcus. The two largest single-point operational risks at HIS are uninsured.
Cyber limit of $1M is light for the customer mix (55% data center, including a 22% hyperscale customer that contractually expects $3M-$5M of cyber).
Georgia PTET has not been elected. At Marcus's projected 2026 K-1 levels, this is approximately $73,000 of recurring annual federal tax savings being left on the table.
Marcus's W-2 is set at $480K with no formal reasonable-compensation analysis on file, leaving ~$5K-$15K of annual FICA optimization unexamined and audit defense thin.
Catherine is not on payroll despite legitimate business contributions — a missed $15K-$25K of annual benefit (deduction, retirement plan capacity, individual DI insurability).

No cost segregation study has been performed on the $4.2M Kennesaw building (gated by the entity separation in Section 1), and no §469(c)(7) grouping has been considered. Once unblocked, year-one benefit is approximately $516K of accelerated depreciation tax savings.
No R&D credit study has been pursued despite the controls and process-piping work that typically qualifies. Combined federal+GA net benefit estimated at $135K-$165K annually.

Existing 401(k) is a generic Safe Harbor plan with no profit-sharing layer, no cross-tested allocation, and no dedicated TPA. Approximately $74K of annual federal tax savings sits unrealized in a properly designed cash-balance overlay, plus $14,615 in profit-sharing optimization.
Catherine's $340K Schwab Rollover IRA blocks her backdoor Roth via the pro-rata rule; a workplace-401(k) rollover would unlock another $8,600/year of tax-free growth capacity.

No formal valuation, Quality of Earnings, or sell-side banker engaged. With two unsolicited approaches already in hand, the cost of going to market without competitive tension or pre-diligence-cleaned books is meaningful — preliminary modeling suggests $1.75M-$5.4M of net banker-driven premium and $1.6M-$7.2M of QofE-driven value preservation.

Top customer concentration runs at 47% (top 3) and 22% (single hyperscale data-center customer). Buyers discount this 15-35% if unaddressed — a $5.25M-$12.25M valuation drag that 24 months of contract conversion and account expansion can largely close.
Management bench gaps below Derek (operations manager, project executive, business development) read to buyers as owner-dependence and apply a separate 20-40% discount unless closed pre-transaction.
No written succession plan for owner death/disability — bank signature authority, customer notification protocol, and operational continuity all sit informally in Marcus's head.
No 529 plans for Olivia, James, or Sophie despite full capacity to deploy a $570K front-load using 5-year averaging.
Eleanor (78) has no LTC insurance and no current POA/healthcare directive set on file with you — projected family exposure of $300K-$1M+ over a multi-year care scenario.

Children have not received structured financial-literacy preparation; given the age-35 restriction philosophy, the runway to align Olivia (19), James (16), and Sophie (12) with the wealth they will eventually steward is real and finite.

No charitable vehicle is in place despite Marcus's stated intent on North Cobb Tech trade scholarships and Catherine's children's-hospital board work. A DAF funded with appreciated taxable-brokerage securities now, plus a pre-LOI gift of non-voting Holdco units, captures roughly $280K of combined federal benefit on a $500K seed and ~$894K on the pre-transaction gift.

recommendations_business.intro_paragraph: The seven business recommendations below run in sequence. The order matters: entity restructuring sets up the tax and estate planning that follows; tax planning funds the insurance program; insurance enables the estate transfers; the estate transfers operate at maximum leverage only if they happen before the transaction window narrows. We have organized the sections so each one builds on the prior.

sections.label: [CORE SECTION]
sections.intro_paragraph: How HIS, the Kennesaw real estate, and Marcus's ownership are titled matters for liability protection, taxation, and the eventual sale of the enterprise. Four structural changes need to happen in sequence over the next 90 to 120 days, and the sequence matters. Done in the right order, the tax cost of the restructuring is essentially zero — direct fees of approximately $40K-$80K against ~$7.4M of valuation discount unlocked on Marcus's non-voting interest alone, before any of the downstream estate planning runs.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Separate the real estate first.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Form Holloway Properties, LLC (Georgia). Transfer the $4.2M Kennesaw facility from HIS to Holloway Properties via a properly documented §721/§351 contribution-and-distribution structure that preserves basis. Put a written, market-rate triple-net lease in place between HIS (tenant) and Holloway Properties (landlord) — engage a third-party broker to opine on FMV rent before execution; the IRS will look at this in any audit. Direct cost approximately $15K-$30K. Truist consent on the $4M revolver is a 0-30 day item.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Execute the F-reorganization to install Holloway Holdings.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Form Holloway Holdings, LLC (Georgia) as the new parent; HIS becomes a QSub. File Form 2553 and Form 8869 with the IRS. The §368(a)(1)(F) treatment is non-recognition — basis carries over, no gain triggered. Direct cost approximately $20K-$35K, but this is the gateway move: every estate and charitable freeze that follows depends on having a holdco above the operating entity. Engage specialist M&A counsel for this — not the general practitioner currently handling ops.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Recapitalize Holdco into voting and non-voting interests.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Inside Holdco, recapitalize Marcus's 88% into voting (~10%) and non-voting (~78%) units. The non-voting interest — approximately $24.75M of FMV pre-discount — is what feeds the GRAT, IDGT, and SLAT planning in Section 4. A qualified appraisal supporting a 25-35% combined LOM/minority discount produces approximately $7.4M of defensible valuation discount (range $6.2M-$8.7M). Derek's consent is required on his 12% — coordinate as part of the replacement operating agreement below.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Replace the operating agreement to reflect the new structure.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: M&A counsel drafts a replacement Holdco operating agreement reflecting the voting/non-voting class structure, transfer restrictions, the cross-purchase buy/sell mechanics from Section 5, and Derek's role and economics through a future transaction. Direct cost ~$5K-$15K now versus $25K-$100K of buyer-driven amendment if you wait until the table.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Build the asset-protection architecture across the three entities.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: With Holloway Properties, Holdco, and HIS in place, isolate operating-business liability from real estate and from the family's economic interest in the enterprise. Coordinate Truist documentation, commercial P&C re-papering, and lease alignment so the structure is operating, not just papered. PSA quarterbacks; cost is largely embedded in the engagements above.

closer_paragraph.label: Why this sequence matters
closer_paragraph.body: If you do the GRAT or IDGT before the recap, you give up the valuation discount and lose meaningful estate-tax savings. If you transfer the real estate after the F-reorg rather than before, the contribution-and-distribution mechanics get more complex. The order is: real estate separation first, F-reorg and holdco second, recap third, replacement operating agreement fourth, then the estate work in Section 4. Done in the right order over 90-120 days, the direct tax cost is essentially zero and the structural value unlocked exceeds $7M before any downstream planning.

sections.label: [CORE SECTION]
sections.intro_paragraph: HIS holds operating cash that is currently parked at near-zero bank yield. The exact balance has not been disclosed by the controller yet, but at any reasonable scale a tiered structure converts idle business cash from sub-1% to 4-5% Treasury or money-market yield without operational risk. On the order of $30K-$170K per year of pre-tax yield uplift depending on the actual balance — meaningful, recurring, and effectively free.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Open a tiered business cash structure.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Three layers: (1) Operating account — 30 days of payables, full liquidity at the existing Truist relationship. (2) Working reserve — 90 days of operating expenses in money market or 1-year laddered T-bills currently yielding ~4.5%. (3) Strategic reserve — funds earmarked for tax payments (PTET, profit-sharing), retention bonuses, and key initiatives, in a higher-yield Treasury or municipal sweep. We will model the right balance across the three based on HIS's billing cycle and seasonality once the controller provides the monthly opex run-rate and the current bank balance.

closer_paragraph.label: Quantified impact
closer_paragraph.body: We are blocked on the precise number until the controller delivers the current HIS bank balance and a 12-month operating cash forecast — a 30-day item. Once that is in hand, we will publish the worked example. The framework holds either way: the difference between a sub-1% operating account and a 4.5% reserve on idle business cash is 350-450 basis points of pre-tax yield that is currently unearned, and on a $1M-$3M idle balance that funds a non-trivial share of the insurance and PTET costs in Sections 3 and 5.

cross_references.display_text: the PTET cash needs in Section 3
the buy/sell premium funding in Section 5

sections.label: [CORE SECTION]
sections.intro_paragraph: Your effective combined federal+Georgia tax rate of approximately 41% is well above what is achievable with PTET, an optimized W-2/K-1 mix, Catherine on payroll, the cost segregation and §469 grouping that the entity separation unlocks, and an R&D credit study. Aggregate annual recurring federal+state savings across the items below run on the order of $250K+ once fully implemented, plus a one-time year-one benefit north of $500K from cost segregation. We have grouped the recommendations into three subsections by timing.

subsections.heading: 3A. Implement This Year
subsections.intro: These four items can and should be executed in 2026. They produce recurring annual savings and require no entity restructuring to capture.

bullets.bold_imperative: File the Georgia PTET election for 2026.
bullets.briefing: On Marcus's projected 2026 K-1 income, Georgia PTET at 5.19% generates approximately $73,000 of recurring annual federal tax savings (range $63K-$77K depending on federal marginal rate). The CPA produces a side-by-side projection, files quarterly Form 602-ES estimated payments via the Georgia Tax Center, and verifies the PTEDED credit appears on Marcus's Form 500. The election is annual — we re-evaluate every year.

bullets.bold_imperative: Optimize Marcus's W-2 / K-1 mix.
bullets.briefing: Engage the CPA for a formal reasonable-compensation analysis. Right-sizing W-2 from $480K toward approximately $360K saves Medicare + additional Medicare on the adjusted portion and adds modest cash-balance plan optimization tailwind. Net annual benefit approximately $5K-$15K. Adjust payroll forward-only — do not retro-adjust — and file the analysis in HIS's tax workpapers for audit defense.

bullets.bold_imperative: Add Catherine to HIS payroll.
bullets.briefing: Document Catherine's role (board work, marketing strategy, family-office function) with a job description, hours, and deliverables. Set salary defensibly at $40K-$80K. Net annual benefit approximately $15K-$25K — business deduction at 42% combined, less FICA, plus 401(k) deferral capacity, plus access to individual disability underwriting that is otherwise unavailable to her. Maintain a quarterly file of deliverables and time logs.

bullets.bold_imperative: Put James and Sophie on payroll for legitimate work.
bullets.briefing: Two children at defensible wage levels (James 16, Sophie 12) generates approximately $6,300 of annual business-level tax savings (range $5K-$8K). More importantly, the wages fund custodial Roth IRAs at Schwab — over a 50-year horizon, projected tax-free growth across both children exceeds $1.8M. Roles must be real, documented, and paid through Paychex like any other employee. See the family lens on custodial Roths.

subsections.heading: 3B. Evaluate Within 12 Months
subsections.intro: These two items are sequenced behind the entity separation in Section 1 — the cost-seg study and the §469 grouping require the Kennesaw real estate to sit inside Holloway Properties first.

bullets.bold_imperative: Commission a cost segregation study on the Kennesaw facility.
bullets.briefing: Once the building has been moved to Holloway Properties (Section 1), engage a cost-segregation engineering firm to reclassify roughly 25-35% of the $4.2M cost basis into 5- and 15-year property. Combined with 100% bonus depreciation under OBBBA and the §469 grouping below, year-one tax savings are approximately $516,000 (range $405K-$605K) at the 42.19% combined federal+GA marginal rate, net of the ~$15K study fee. The CPA implements via Form 4562 and Form 3115 §481(a) catch-up.

bullets.bold_imperative: File the §469(c)(7) grouping election.
bullets.briefing: Without the grouping, the cost-seg-driven depreciation is passive-trapped against Marcus's active K-1 income — and the year-one benefit collapses. The grouping election unlocks approximately $531,594 of year-one federal+GA tax savings on the accelerated depreciation against active income (range $425K-$620K). Confirm common ownership and material participation in both Holloway Properties and HIS, then file with the return for the grouping year. Maintain time logs going forward.

subsections.heading: 3C. Long-Term Considerations
subsections.intro: One specialty item under evaluation; recurring once established.

bullets.bold_imperative: Pursue an R&D credit study.
bullets.briefing: HIS has not pursued R&D credits historically despite the controls integration and process-piping work that typically qualifies. Engage a specialty firm for a no-cost feasibility review; if positive, the full study runs 4-6 months. Estimated annual federal + Georgia credit net of contingent fee is $135K-$165K (range $40K-$165K depending on the QRE quantification at $1.5M-ish). The CPA incorporates via Form 6765 and the state R&D form, and we establish contemporaneous documentation discipline so the credit recurs annually.

closer_paragraph.label: Quantified impact
closer_paragraph.body: Annualized recurring savings across this section approach $230K-$300K once 3A and 3C are fully implemented (PTET ~$73K + W-2 mix ~$10K + Catherine ~$20K + family wages ~$6K + R&D ~$150K). Layered on top, the year-one cost-seg + §469 combination produces a one-time benefit of roughly $1.0M-$1.1M. The PTET savings alone fund the entire annual cost of the buy/sell life insurance program in Section 5 with room to spare.

sections.label: [CORE SECTION]
sections.intro_paragraph: Projected federal estate tax exposure is approximately $11.4M – $17.6M today and growing with the business. The 2014 wills do not address it; there are no trusts in place, no annual exclusion gifting program, no GST allocation. The strategy is layered — foundation documents first, annual exclusion gifts second, leveraged transfers (GRAT, IDGT, SLAT) third, GST allocation across all of them. The combined estate-tax savings across the layers run to the eight figures, with the highest-leverage moves running before the transaction window narrows.

subsections.heading: 4A. Foundation — File This Quarter
subsections.intro: The foundation documents are not optional and not expensive. They are the substrate every other estate move depends on.

bullets.bold_imperative: Stand up a joint revocable living trust and pour-over wills.
bullets.briefing: Estate attorney drafts the joint revocable trust, pour-over wills naming the trust as residual beneficiary, and funds the trust by retitling investment accounts, real estate, and (post-recap) Marcus's voting interest. Probate avoidance on the ~$5M titled probate estate saves approximately $50K-$150K of Georgia attorney fees and 9-18 months of court timeline.

bullets.bold_imperative: Execute the full document set: POA, healthcare directive, HIPAA, guardianship.
bullets.briefing: Attorney drafts DPOAs and healthcare directives for both Marcus and Catherine, HIPAA authorizations, and guardianship designations naming Sophie's guardian (the 2014 wills do not). Adult-child HIPAA and healthcare POA for Olivia at UGA. Direct attorney fee approximately $8K-$18K; avoids $5K-$25K of conservatorship cost on any incapacity event plus weeks of court delay. Notarize and witness per Georgia law.

bullets.bold_imperative: Run a beneficiary-designation audit across all accounts.
bullets.briefing: PSA-led pass over 401(k), Rollover IRA, life policies, and bank/investment accounts to confirm beneficiary designations match the new estate plan. Stale designations override wills and routinely defeat 10+ years of planning. This is a 0-30 day, no-fee item.

subsections.heading: 4B. Annual Exclusion + Children's Trusts
subsections.intro: The receiving structure for every gift, GRAT remainder, and IDGT distribution that follows.

bullets.bold_imperative: Stand up three children's trusts as the receiving structure.
bullets.briefing: One per child (Olivia, James, Sophie), drafted with HEMS distribution standards, age-35 restriction language matching your hard rule, GST-exempt drafting, and Crummey-noticed gift mechanics. Corporate trustee preferred for institutional independence and continuity. Cumulative federal estate tax savings as the trusts accumulate annual gifts + GRAT remainder + IDGT distributions + growth: approximately $4M – $8M (midpoint ~$6M).

bullets.bold_imperative: Calendar an annual exclusion gifting program in January.
bullets.briefing: $152K/year of capacity for the Holloways: 4 donees × 2 spouses × $19K. Over a 20-year program that is approximately $3.04M of estate removal at a 40% federal estate tax rate — approximately $1.216M of estate tax avoided on the gifts alone, range $1.2M-$2.6M including compound growth on gifted assets. CPA files Form 709 annually with the gift-splitting election; estate attorney handles Crummey notice mechanics for the trust gifts.

subsections.heading: 4C. Leveraged Transfers — Run These Inside the Transaction Window
subsections.intro: These are the highest-leverage moves in the plan. They depend on the recap (Section 1) and a qualified appraisal of the non-voting interest at funding. The remainder beneficiary is the children's trusts above.

bullets.bold_imperative: Fund a 3-Year Zeroed-Out GRAT with ~$8.2M of non-voting Holdco units.
bullets.briefing: The GRAT pays an annuity back to Marcus over 3 years sized so the gift-tax value of the remainder is zero at the §7520 hurdle rate (~5.0% currently). Any growth above the hurdle rate passes to the children's trusts free of gift and estate tax. At HIS's recent growth profile, expected remainder transferred is approximately $4.2M (range $0 if business stalls to $6.5M+ if growth continues or a transaction lands during the term). Qualified appraisal at funding is essential.

bullets.bold_imperative: Execute the IDGT installment sale.
bullets.briefing: Seed the Intentionally Defective Grantor Trust with ~10% of intended sale value (~$1.75M) using lifetime exemption, then sell ~$25M of non-voting Holdco units (pre-discount) to the IDGT for a 9-year AFR-rate installment note. "Defective" is a term of art — Marcus pays the trust's income tax under §675(4) grantor-trust treatment, which is itself a tax-free gift to the trust each year (Rev. Rul. 85-13). Estimated additional value transferred outside the estate over the note term + transaction: approximately $8M-$15M (midpoint ~$10M).

bullets.bold_imperative: Fund a Spousal Lifetime Access Trust (SLAT) for Catherine.
bullets.briefing: Estate attorney drafts a SLAT for Catherine and the children with a HEMS distribution standard, independent trustee, and §2036-safe drafting. Fund with ~$5M of an appreciating asset (real estate, business interest, or securities). Over a 25-year horizon at 7% growth, approximately $10.8M of federal estate tax avoided (range $5.4M-$16.2M depending on growth and horizon). Catherine retains indirect access via distributions from the trust, which is the key feature for a couple this side of liquidity.

bullets.bold_imperative: Allocate GST exemption explicitly across all dynasty-eligible trusts.
bullets.briefing: Without explicit GST allocation on Form 709 at funding, transfers to children's trusts and IDGT default to a non-zero inclusion ratio — meaning eventual distributions to grandchildren get hit with 40% generation-skipping transfer tax. Decide trust situs (Georgia is RAP-limited; Delaware, South Dakota, or Nevada permit perpetual dynasty trusts). On $30M allocated to a perpetual dynasty trust growing 5% over 60 years, the GST tax avoided is approximately $224M at the midpoint (range $50M-$500M depending on growth and horizon).

closer_paragraph.label: Combined estate impact
closer_paragraph.body: Layered together, the children's trusts ($4M-$8M), GRAT remainder (~$4.2M midpoint), IDGT transfer ($8M-$15M), SLAT ($5.4M-$16.2M), and annual exclusion gifting ($1.2M-$2.6M) eliminate the bulk of the projected $11.4M-$17.6M federal estate tax exposure. GST allocation extends that benefit to grandchildren and beyond. The ILIT-owned life insurance in Section 5 funds whatever residual estate tax remains so heirs do not face a forced sale of business interests at the worst possible moment.

sections.label: [CORE SECTION]
sections.intro_paragraph: Insurance is one of the few financial decisions where the right answer is shaped by exposure, not opinion. Our approach is to size protection to specific dollar exposures, not generic rules of thumb. Across HIS today there are five business-side gaps: no buy/sell funding between Marcus and Derek, no ILIT-owned estate liquidity, no business overhead expense coverage on Marcus, no key-person coverage on Derek, and a $1M cyber limit that is light for the data-center mix.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Fund the cross-purchase buy/sell between Marcus and Derek.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Marcus owns approximately $5M of life insurance on Derek (covers Derek's 12% × $42M midpoint = $5.04M). Derek owns a $5M starting layer on Marcus, with Marcus's $36.96M residual interest covered by the ILIT-owned estate-liquidity layer below. Premium funded via bonus-and-loan to Derek for the asymmetric portion. Aggregate annual premium estimated $20K-$40K. M&A counsel drafts the cross-purchase agreement so terms mirror the replacement operating agreement in Section 1 — both owners undergo medical underwriting in parallel.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Stand up the ILIT and bind ~$10M of survivorship UL.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Estate attorney drafts the ILIT first; the ILIT is policyowner and beneficiary from inception (do not buy personally and try to gift in — three-year lookback issues). Insurance broker underwrites Marcus and Catherine for $10M Survivorship UL (range $8M-$12M depending on final estate-tax projection). Death proceeds keep approximately $3.6M of federal estate tax outside the taxable estate at the second death, with cushion for the ILIT remainder to grandchildren. Annual Crummey-noticed premium funding through the ILIT.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Underwrite Business Overhead Expense (BOE) coverage on Marcus.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: BOE covers HIS's fixed overhead (rent, payroll, utilities, professional, debt service) during a Marcus disability — preserving enterprise value through a 12-24 month recovery. Annual premium $3K-$8K, business-owned. CPA documents the fixed overhead inventory to size the monthly maximum.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Bind ~$2M of key-person term coverage on Derek.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: HIS owns and is beneficiary of a $2M term policy on Derek, sized to 12-18 months of transition disruption to enterprise value pre-transaction (range $1M-$3M). Execute §101(j) notice-and-consent with Derek BEFORE policy issuance — this is non-negotiable; without it, the death benefit is taxable. CPA files Form 8925 annually for employer-owned life insurance reporting.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Raise cyber liability to $3M-$5M.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: The current $1M cyber limit is light for HIS's customer mix — particularly the 22% hyperscale data-center customer that contractually expects $3M-$5M of cyber. Quote $3M-$5M with a specialty cyber broker; expected annual premium $3K-$8K. Verify ransomware payment provisions and triggers, and coordinate with HIS IT on incident response protocols.

closer_paragraph.label: Why this sequence matters
closer_paragraph.body: The buy/sell, ILIT, and key-person items move together because they share underwriting, attorney engagements, and policy timing — running them in parallel saves 60-90 days versus serial execution. The aggregate annual premium load across the full business-side insurance program runs roughly $30K-$60K, which is approximately what the PTET election in Section 3 saves on its own. The unfunded buy/sell is the single largest unfunded liability on the business balance sheet — closing it should not wait.

the ILIT structure standing behind the survivorship policy (Section 4)
the PTET savings that fund the program (Section 3)

sections.label: [CORE SECTION]
sections.intro_paragraph: The HIS 401(k) is a generic Safe Harbor plan with no profit-sharing layer, no cross-tested allocation, and no dedicated TPA. Properly designed, the same plan supports Marcus's 401(k) deferral, a profit-sharing layer to §415(c) cap, and a cash-balance overlay — together producing approximately $100K of recurring annual federal tax savings for Marcus alone. Layered with a §409A-compliant SERP for Derek and the controller, the plan does double duty as the retention structure that holds the bench together through a transaction.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Maximize Marcus's 401(k) deferral including catch-up.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Confirm Marcus is at the 2026 cap of $32,500 ($23K base + $7.5K age-50 catch-up) — approximately $12,025 of recurring annual federal tax savings at his marginal rate (range $10.5K-$13.3K). Coordinate with Empower on the SECURE 2.0 Roth catch-up requirement: at $480K of FICA wages (above the $150K threshold), Marcus's $7,500 catch-up must be Roth. Verify the plan supports it; if not, push the amendment.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Add a cash-balance plan layered on the 401(k).
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Engage a DB-specialist TPA to design a cash-balance plan with Marcus's pay credit at the §415-permitted level and a workforce analysis across HIS's 87 FTEs to project the compliance allocation cost for non-owner employees. Marcus's pay credit ~$200K/year produces approximately $74,000 of annual federal tax savings (range $55K-$111K). ERISA counsel drafts the plan document; pursue an IRS determination letter for the first plan year.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Add a cross-tested profit-sharing layer to the 401(k).
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Amend the plan document for a cross-tested profit-sharing formula. Marcus's allocation ~$39,500/year fills him to the §415(c) $72,000 cap and produces approximately $14,615 of annual federal tax savings (range $12.8K-$16.2K). TPA runs annual nondiscrimination testing across the 87 FTEs; HIS funds by tax-filing deadline including extensions.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Amend the plan document to support the design above.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: The current Empower plan document does not support the cash-balance overlay or the SECURE 2.0 Roth catch-up. PSA quarterbacks the amendment with the TPA and Empower. This is a 0-30 day prerequisite — the contributions and tax savings above are blocked until the document is updated.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Confirm SECURE 2.0 Roth catch-up compliance for Marcus.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: At $480K FICA wages, Marcus's $7,500 (or $8,000 in 2026) catch-up must be categorized as Roth. Verify with Empower that the plan supports it and that payroll is coding correctly. No economic loss — just tax-treatment change to tax-free growth. Compliance gap closure, not a new dollar line.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Stand up a §409A-compliant SERP for Derek and the controller.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Supplemental Executive Retirement Plan with informal COLI funding — combined annual COLI premium approximately $120K ($80K Derek + $40K controller). 7-year cliff vesting tied to transaction or service. Specialist NQDC counsel drafts the plan document and participant agreements; insurance broker places the COLI policies after §101(j) notice-and-consent. The SERP is the retention spine alongside the stay-bonus pool in Section 7 — together they signal to Derek and the bench that the upside through a transition is real.

closer_paragraph.label: Quantified impact
closer_paragraph.body: Combined annual federal tax savings across the 401(k) deferral, cash-balance, and profit-sharing layers run approximately $100,640 for Marcus at midpoint ($12,025 + $74,000 + $14,615), recurring. The SERP costs HIS approximately $120K/year of COLI funding but is paired against the multimillion-dollar value of holding Derek and the controller through the transaction window — the same window in which the stay-bonus pool ($320K-$1.44M) and the management bench build (Section 7) close the owner-dependence discount that buyers otherwise apply at 20-40% of transaction value.

cross_references.display_text: the stay-bonus pool and bench build (Section 7)
the W-2 mix that interacts with cash-balance design (Section 3)

sections.label: [OPTIONAL — included because of pre-transaction posture]
sections.intro_paragraph: Two unsolicited approaches in the past 14 months mean the transaction window may be closer than the stated three-to-five-year horizon. The work below is what separates a reactive sale from a competitive process — value preservation, value creation, and value protection through close. The aggregate dollar gap between a well-prepared and a reactive transaction on a $35M midpoint enterprise runs into the eight figures.

subsections.heading: 7A. Pre-Transaction Value Preservation (T-18 to T-12 months)
subsections.intro: These items run 12-18 months before going to market. Each one closes a discrete buyer-side discount.

bullets.bold_imperative: Engage a fractional CFO 12-18 months pre-transaction.
bullets.briefing: Fractional CFO at approximately $80K-$200K/year over 18 months closes the finance-function gap pre-diligence. Working-capital optimization, GAAP-clean accounting, and re-trade avoidance routinely return 5-10% of transaction value relative to cost. Specific dollar varies with engagement intensity and firm; the framework is firm.

bullets.bold_imperative: Commission a sell-side Quality of Earnings.
bullets.briefing: Engage a QofE firm independent of the existing CPA 12-18 months pre-transaction. Cost approximately $75K-$125K. Expected transaction-value preservation 5-15% on the $32M-$48M range = approximately $1.6M-$7.2M (midpoint ~$4M). The QofE finds re-trade items before the buyer does, normalizes accounting, and documents customer concentration in a defensible format. Refresh 6 months later if material time elapses before going to market.

bullets.bold_imperative: Close the management bench gaps.
bullets.briefing: Below Derek, the gaps to close pre-transaction are an operations manager, a project executive, and a business development lead. Buyers read these gaps as owner-dependence and apply a 20-40% discount unless closed. PSA partners with HIS on org analysis, sourcing strategy, compensation packages, and milestone tracking through the 12-24 month build.

bullets.bold_imperative: De-risk customer concentration on the top hyperscale customer.
bullets.briefing: 47% top-3 / 22% top-1 concentration translates to a buyer-applied discount of 15-35% — approximately $5.25M-$12.25M of valuation drag on the $35M midpoint (midpoint ~$8.75M). The two-handed fix is a 3-year long-term contract with the hyperscale data-center customer (converts concentration to predictability) plus a quarterly KPI dashboard tracking top-1, top-3, and top-10 mix as sales-team incentives shift toward diversification.

subsections.heading: 7B. Retention & Continuity
subsections.intro: Two items that hold the team through the transaction and protect operations against any owner-incapacity event in the meantime.

bullets.bold_imperative: Stand up a stay-bonus pool sized at 1-3% of expected transaction value.
bullets.briefing: Pool of approximately $320K-$1.44M (midpoint $800K at 2% of $40M) for Derek, the controller, and the project-management leads. Half payable at close, half at the 12-month post-close anniversary. M&A counsel drafts agreements with closing trigger, vesting tail, and separation provisions. Communicate confidentially on banker timing — too early creates premature transaction signaling, too late and the retention purpose is lost.

bullets.bold_imperative: Document a written succession plan for owner death/disability.
bullets.briefing: Written plan covering interim leadership designation, bank signature authority at Truist, customer notification protocols, and operational continuity. Currently sits informally in Marcus's head; a single event makes that paralysis. PSA leads inventory and designation; Truist updates signature cards; business attorney distributes the document set.

subsections.heading: 7C. Banker & Pre-LOI Charitable Move (T-12 months)
subsections.intro: The banker selection and the pre-LOI gift of non-voting Holdco units are the last big pre-market moves.

bullets.bold_imperative: Engage a sell-side M&A banker 12 months before going to market.
bullets.briefing: Shortlist 2-3 finalists with documented HVAC/MEP industry specialty, run reference checks, and execute the engagement letter with M&A counsel reviewing success-fee structure, minimums, and carve-outs. Net competitive premium captured 15-30% on the preliminary $35M midpoint, less ~1-2% banker fees, equals approximately $1.75M-$5.4M of net banker-driven premium (midpoint ~$3.5M). The two unsolicited approaches are leverage in a competitive process, not a substitute for one.

bullets.bold_imperative: Execute the pre-LOI gift of non-voting Holdco units to a charitable vehicle.
bullets.briefing: Before any binding LOI is signed, gift approximately 5% of non-voting Holdco units (range 3%-7%) to a DAF or family foundation. The combined federal benefit on a 5% gift at the $42M scenario with 30% discount: approximately $544K of federal deduction value at 37% plus $350K of capital-gains avoidance at 23.8% = approximately $894K (range $600K-$1.2M). Charity ultimately receives ~$2.1M at full transaction value. The anticipatory-assignment-of-income gate is the binding LOI — before it, this works; after it, it doesn't. Qualified USPAP-compliant valuation is essential.

closer_paragraph.label: Combined estate impact
closer_paragraph.body: The pre-transaction work is collectively the highest-leverage block in the plan. QofE preservation $1.6M-$7.2M, banker premium $1.75M-$5.4M, concentration-discount recovery $5.25M-$12.25M, plus the bench build closing the owner-dependence discount — against direct costs of roughly $200K-$400K. Add the $894K combined federal benefit on the pre-LOI charitable gift. The window to do this work is 12-24 months; the cost of compressing it because an inbound buyer accelerates is meaningful, which is why we are building it now.

cross_references.display_text: the entity structure that the banker and QofE rely on (Section 1)
the SERP and stay-bonus pool together as the retention spine (Section 6)
the IDGT and GRAT planning that should run before the transaction window (Section 4)

recommendations_personal.intro_paragraph: The business is the first place we invest. Personal portfolios, personal risk, and personal estate planning are the second — they are what give you flexibility, independence, and a soft landing if anything happens to the business. With approximately 80%+ of household net worth concentrated in HIS today, the personal lens is about deliberate diversification, structural protection of what is already outside the business, and using the next three to five years to build the infrastructure that a post-transaction balance sheet will need. The five sections below address personal cash, personal risk, personal investment, family and education, and personal estate planning — in that order.

sections.label: [PERSONAL — for owner(s)]
sections.intro_paragraph: Personal cash is the smallest piece of the household balance sheet, but it is the easiest to fix and it sets a hygiene standard for everything that follows. The joint checking account at Truist holds approximately $310,000 — appropriate for a household with $35K–$40K of monthly outflows and irregular K-1 distribution timing, but more than is needed in a 0.1% checking account. Layering the cash into operating, emergency, and near-term tiers produces an estimated $12,500 per year of additional yield (range $8,000–$18,000 depending on prevailing HYSA and Treasury rates) — at zero operational risk and at the cost of opening two accounts.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Layer the personal cash into three tiers.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Tier 1 — operating account: 1–2 months of outflows (~$50K) at Truist for full liquidity. Tier 2 — emergency reserve: 6–12 months of outflows in a high-yield savings account or brokerage money market currently yielding ~4.5%. Tier 3 — near-term reserve: funds earmarked for tax payments, planned purchases, and 12–36 month horizon needs in a short Treasury ladder. Net yield uplift on the $260K shifted out of checking is approximately $11,700 per year before the Treasury ladder, ~$12,500 total.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Re-test the layer sizing quarterly.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Cash needs change with K-1 timing, estimated tax payments, and any planned large outlays (Catherine's real estate vision, Eleanor's care support). PSA will re-check layer sizes quarterly and rebalance against prevailing yields so the structure does not silently drift back to a single account.

closer_paragraph.label: Quantified impact
closer_paragraph.body: Approximately $12,500 per year of additional pre-tax yield (range $8,000–$18,000), captured by moving idle checking balances into a tiered structure with no change to liquidity for the operating tier. Small in absolute terms relative to the rest of this plan, but it is a clean discipline that mirrors the business cash work in Section 2 and signals how the household will manage a much larger post-transaction cash position.

cross_references.display_text: the parallel business cash structure in Section 2

sections.label: [PERSONAL — for owner(s)]
sections.intro_paragraph: Insurance is one of the few financial decisions where the right answer is shaped by exposure, not opinion. On the personal side, three exposures are sized incorrectly today: the umbrella ($2M against a multi-million-dollar net worth and a multi-tens-of-millions business equity stake), the auto liability sub-limits ($250K/$250K — below most carriers' umbrella attachment requirements), and individual disability coverage (none — the existing 60% group LTD caps out at a fraction of Marcus's actual income). A fourth exposure, long-term care, is not a gap today but is one we should solve while underwriting is still favorable at ages 52 and 49.

subsections.heading: 9A. Umbrella and Underlying Auto/Home Liability
subsections.intro: The umbrella is the single largest personal-risk gap on the balance sheet. A $2M policy against a household with $25M+ of personal net worth and a much larger business equity stake is not protection — it is a deductible. This is the #1 priority item on the entire plan precisely because a serious auto liability event today could reach into business equity to satisfy a judgment.

bullets.bold_imperative: Bind a $25M personal umbrella from a specialty HNW carrier.
bullets.briefing: Quote at $10M, $25M, and $50M tiers from Chubb, AIG, and Pure. Recommended target: $25M, sized to current personal net worth plus a cushion for income-earning power; revisit to $50M post-transaction. Confirm UM/UIM coverage is included. Aggregate impact: $25M of liability protection against a current $2M policy — a 12.5x increase in coverage for a typical premium of $1,500–$3,000 per year. This is the highest-leverage single decision in the plan.

bullets.bold_imperative: Raise auto and home underlying liability to satisfy umbrella attachment.
bullets.briefing: Current auto liability at $250K/$250K is below what HNW carriers will accept as the underlying for a $25M umbrella. Raise auto to $500K/$500K and home liability to $1M before the umbrella binds; otherwise the umbrella has a coverage gap at the bottom. PSA will coordinate with the new broker to package both moves so they bind on the same effective date.

subsections.heading: 9B. Individual Disability Insurance
subsections.intro: Marcus's current group LTD provides 60% income replacement, capped well below his actual W-2 + K-1 income, with a 24-month own-occupation definition that converts to any-occupation thereafter. For an owner whose income depends on continuing to run HIS, this is meaningfully under-protective.

bullets.bold_imperative: Layer in $25K/month individual own-occupation DI on Marcus.
bullets.briefing: Target ~$25K/month benefit (range $15K–$25K depending on issue limits at HNW underwriting) with own-occupation to age 65/67. Pay premium personally so benefits are received tax-free if claimed. PSA will engage MassMutual through our multi-life relationship for preferred rates. Estimated annual premium: $15K–$25K; closes a $160K–$200K/year replacement-income gap relative to the group LTD.

subsections.heading: 9C. Long-Term Care
subsections.intro: Long-term care is not an immediate crisis at ages 52 and 49 — but underwriting is materially better now than at 60+, and standalone LTC is increasingly expensive and use-it-or-lose-it. A hybrid LTC-life product solves the LTC exposure without the standalone product's inefficiency.

bullets.bold_imperative: Choose hybrid LTC life over standalone LTC.
bullets.briefing: We compared standalone LTC against hybrid LTC-life across a 30-year horizon. Hybrid wins for an HNW couple because the death benefit guarantees the premium dollars are not forfeited if LTC is never used. Recommended structure: single-pay $100K per spouse (range $100K–$300K combined depending on LTC pool size and which spouse(s) covered), funding ~$400K–$700K of LTC pool per spouse plus a $200K–$300K death benefit. Bind through MassMutual.

closer_paragraph.label: Why this sequence matters
closer_paragraph.body: The umbrella binds first because the exposure is live every time anyone in the household drives a car. The auto/home underlying limits move on the same effective date so there is no attachment gap. The individual DI engages 30–60 days behind, on Marcus's underwriting timeline. The hybrid LTC decision can wait 4–6 months — but underwriting only gets harder with age and any health event, so we want it done inside the first plan year while you are both at your most insurable.

cross_references.display_text: the buy/sell life insurance program in Section 5
the ILIT-owned estate liquidity coverage in Section 12

sections.label: [PERSONAL — for owner(s)]
sections.intro_paragraph: The business is the first place you invest. Personal portfolios are the second — they are what give you flexibility, independence, and a soft landing if anything happens to the business. With a 79%–85% concentration ratio in HIS today, the priority through the transaction window is deliberate diversification and tax-aware structure of what is already outside the business. The taxable brokerage at Schwab ($1.8M) becomes PSA-managed, and three changes to how it is invested produce roughly $40,000–$50,000 per year of after-tax alpha at the household's effective combined rate.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Convert the $1.8M taxable brokerage to a direct indexing SMA.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Replace the current S&P-500-equivalent fund with a direct indexing strategy via Aperio, Parametric, or PSA's preferred SMA platform — separately managed account replicating the index using the underlying stocks. The strategy systematically harvests losses on individual positions while maintaining index-like return; typical tax alpha is 50–100 bps annually, approximately $13,500 per year on the $1.8M balance at the midpoint (range $9,000–$18,000). Particularly valuable in years with significant K-1 income to absorb realized capital losses against. Migrate gradually if there are embedded gains.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Implement asset-location optimization across taxable, 401(k), and Catherine's IRA.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Tax-inefficient assets (REITs, taxable bonds, high-turnover strategies) belong in retirement accounts; tax-efficient assets (broad equity, munis, direct-indexed equity) belong in the taxable account. On a combined ~$3.06M of household investable assets, asset-location alpha at ~50 bps is approximately $15,000 per year (range $10,000–$25,000). Constrained somewhat by the Empower 401(k) menu — we will work within those constraints.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Allocate fixed income to municipal bonds, Georgia-specific where attractive.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: At a combined federal+GA effective rate near 41%, taxable corporate fixed income is structurally inferior to munis on an after-tax basis. Once we finalize the Investment Policy Statement and target allocation with you, the fixed-income sleeve in the taxable account moves to munis, with a tilt toward Georgia issuers for the additional state-tax exemption. Specific dollar uplift depends on the IPS allocation decision still to come.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Run quarterly tax-loss harvesting cross-account.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Even with direct indexing handling intra-position harvesting inside the SMA, PSA will run a quarterly review across all taxable holdings, with explicit wash-sale awareness across Catherine's Rollover IRA and the 401(k). Year-end coordination with the CPA to match harvested losses against realized gains. Tax alpha overlaps with the direct indexing line above — we are not double-counting; we are describing the operational discipline that delivers the $13,500/year.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Execute Marcus's annual backdoor Roth and evaluate clearing Catherine's pro-rata block.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Marcus has clean backdoor Roth capacity at $8,600 per year (2026 limit + age-50 catch-up). Catherine is currently blocked by the pro-rata rule because of her $340K Schwab Rollover IRA. Once Catherine is on payroll (Section 3) and enrolled in the HIS 401(k), we evaluate rolling her Rollover IRA into the 401(k) to clear pro-rata and unlock her own backdoor Roth — doubling annual capacity to $17,200.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Hold the line on diversification discipline through the transaction window.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Resist the pre-IPO pattern of moving the personal portfolio aggressively into private markets or single-stock concentration before a sale. Your concentration risk is already very high through the business itself — the personal portfolio should be the diversification, not another concentration. Pre-transaction, the brokerage is the rehearsal infrastructure for managing the much larger post-transaction balance sheet; the discipline we install now scales when the liquidity event closes.

closer_paragraph.label: Quantified impact
closer_paragraph.body: Direct indexing tax alpha approximately $13,500/year, asset location approximately $15,000/year, plus the muni allocation uplift once finalized — combined after-tax personal investment alpha on the order of $30,000–$45,000 per year on the current $3M household investable balance. The alpha scales with the balance, which is the point: the structure is built to absorb a 10x-larger balance after a transaction without rebuilding.

cross_references.display_text: Catherine's payroll onboarding in Section 3
the household retirement strategy in Section 6

sections.label: [PERSONAL — for owner(s)]
sections.intro_paragraph: Education funding, family governance, aging-parent support, and philanthropy all belong on the same page because they share one premise: wealth that arrives on the next generation without context creates problems. The hard family rule — no unrestricted access before age 35 — is enforced structurally through the children's trusts in Section 12; this section funds the legitimate near-term needs (education, productive work, charitable formation) and builds the governance scaffolding that lets the children develop into responsible stewards over time. Eleanor's anticipated care is the fourth piece — the most likely near-term draw on the household's cash flow that is not already in the plan.

bullets.bold_imperative: Front-load three Georgia Path2College 529s in Year 1.
bullets.briefing: Open one 529 per child (Olivia 19, James 16, Sophie 12) on Georgia's Path2College 529 to capture the state deduction. Execute the Year-1 5-year averaging election: $190K per beneficiary using gift-splitting between Marcus and Catherine — $570K total deployed up front. CPA files Form 709 with the 5-year averaging election for each spouse on each beneficiary, and we coordinate with the annual exclusion gifting plan in Section 12 to avoid over-contribution in years 2–5.

bullets.bold_imperative: Open custodial Roth IRAs for James and Sophie funded by HIS wages.
bullets.briefing: Once James and Sophie's roles, hours, and wage rates are documented at HIS (Section 3), open custodial Roth IRAs at Schwab and fund up to earned-income limit ($7,500 each in 2026). One $7,500 contribution at age 16 at 7% growth compounds to roughly $215K by age 65 — tax-free. Pair with documentation discipline: time logs, deliverables, payroll records. The custodial Roth is small in absolute dollars but it is one of the most powerful long-horizon vehicles available.

bullets.bold_imperative: Build a care-and-finance plan for Eleanor before it becomes urgent.
bullets.briefing: Eleanor (78, Marietta, currently independent and in good health) is the most likely near-term household financial obligation outside the plan as written. The work: Marcus opens the conversation with Eleanor on wishes and care preferences; PSA compiles her financial picture (assets, Social Security, any existing insurance); we model home care, assisted living, and skilled nursing scenarios across a range of $300K–$1M+ multi-year cost. Specific gap to the Holloways depends on Eleanor's resources.

bullets.bold_imperative: Evaluate LTC insurance for Eleanor while she is still potentially insurable.
bullets.briefing: At 78 and in good health, Eleanor may still be insurable for a meaningful LTC product — that window narrows quickly. Health Insurance Broker engages on a feasibility evaluation; if economics are unfavorable at her age, we set a self-funding reserve in the household plan instead. Either path is fine; what matters is making the decision consciously rather than discovering the gap when care becomes necessary.

bullets.bold_imperative: Update Eleanor's POA, healthcare directive, and HIPAA authorization.
bullets.briefing: Estate Attorney drafts and Eleanor executes a current durable POA, healthcare directive, and HIPAA authorization naming Marcus (and Catherine as alternate). Without these, a sudden incapacity event triggers conservatorship — which is expensive, slow, and entirely avoidable. Pair this with the same document set for Marcus, Catherine, and Olivia (now 19) being drafted in Section 12.

subsections.heading: 11C. Philanthropy — Near-Term DAF, Pre-Transaction Business Gift
subsections.intro: Catherine's hard preference is a family foundation post-liquidity; the DAF is the right near-term vehicle and pairs with a much larger pre-transaction gift of business interest that is one of the most tax-efficient single moves available before a sale.

bullets.bold_imperative: Open a DAF and fund it with appreciated brokerage securities.
bullets.briefing: Choose Fidelity Charitable, Schwab Charitable, or a community foundation as sponsor. Marcus and Catherine as primary advisors; Olivia, James, and Sophie as successor advisors — this is the values-formation mechanism Catherine specifically asked for. Fund initially with appreciated taxable-brokerage securities rather than cash to capture both the deduction and the capital-gains avoidance. On a representative $500K appreciated-stock contribution, combined federal benefit is approximately $280,000 ($185K deduction at 37% + $95K cap-gains avoidance at 23.8%). Annual giving target: $50K–$100K from the DAF to the children's hospital and North Cobb Tech scholarships.

bullets.bold_imperative: Gift ~5% of non-voting Holdco units to the DAF before any binding LOI.
bullets.briefing: Pre-transaction gift of business interest is one of the most leveraged charitable moves available — but only if executed before a binding letter of intent, which is the IRS anticipatory-assignment-of-income gate. Mechanics: qualified USPAP appraisal of non-voting units with discounts; formal gift documentation; transfer to DAF; CPA files Form 8283 with appraisal attachment. On a ~5% gift at the $42M high end of the valuation range with 30% discount, combined federal benefit is approximately $894,000 ($544K deduction at 37% + $350K cap-gains avoidance at 23.8%); the charity ultimately receives ~$2.1M at full transaction value. Range $600K–$1.2M depending on final business value and gift percent.

subsections.heading: 11D. Family Governance
subsections.intro: The trust structure in Section 12 enforces the age-35 rule legally; family governance is what builds the children's capacity to handle the wealth when those restrictions release.

bullets.bold_imperative: Draft a family mission statement.
bullets.briefing: PSA facilitates a working session with Marcus and Catherine to codify the values that govern household wealth — the dignity-of-skilled-trades thread, the children's hospital and trade scholarship causes, the age-35 philosophy, the Derek-loyalty premise. The output is a 1–2 page document that anchors trustee discretion, supports next-gen alignment, and gets attached as an exhibit to the trust documents and the eventual foundation charter. Qualitative benefit, but it is the document everyone returns to when there is conflict 20 years from now.

bullets.bold_imperative: Hold an annual family meeting; build a multi-year financial-education curriculum.
bullets.briefing: First family meeting within 6–12 months: Marcus and Catherine present what the household is doing and why, age-appropriate to each child. Curriculum: Olivia (19) is ready for the operational layer — how the trusts work, what her HIPAA and healthcare POA do, what a DAF is. James (16) gets the budgeting + custodial Roth conversation. Sophie (12) gets values-and-giving conversations. The point is deliberate, paced communication — not a single download — so the family wealth conversation evolves rather than ambushing the children later.

closer_paragraph.label: What this means
closer_paragraph.body: $570K of education funding deployed up front for the three children, a custodial-Roth structure in place for James and Sophie, an LTC and document plan for Eleanor before crisis forces a worse decision, a DAF that absorbs $50K–$100K of annual giving with associated federal benefit, and — the largest single number on this page — a pre-transaction business interest gift that produces approximately $894,000 of combined federal benefit while delivering a much larger amount to the charity. The governance work runs underneath all of it and is what makes the structure last.

sections.label: [PERSONAL — for owner(s)]
sections.intro_paragraph: The current estate plan dates from 2014, when household net worth was approximately $3M. Today the projected estate-tax exposure is $11.4M–$17.6M, the wills do not name a guardian for Sophie, there is no revocable trust, no ILIT, no DPOA, no healthcare directive, and Olivia turned 19 without a HIPAA authorization. The work below is layered: foundation documents in the first 30 days, annual-exclusion gifting and children's trusts in 30–60 days, ILIT and SUL in 60–120 days, GRAT and SLAT in 6–12 months, IDGT and GST/dynasty layer in 12–24 months. Done in sequence, the cumulative estate-tax savings range across the layers is on the order of $20M+ at the midpoint, with substantial additional GST capacity across the multi-generational horizon.

subsections.heading: 12A. Foundation Documents — First 30–60 Days
subsections.intro: These are the documents that should already exist. They get drafted first because they are prerequisites for everything else and because they close acute exposures (no guardian for Sophie, no DPOA, no healthcare directive) that have nothing to do with sophistication and everything to do with hygiene.

bullets.bold_imperative: Establish a joint revocable living trust and pour-over wills.
bullets.briefing: Estate Attorney drafts a joint revocable trust for Marcus and Catherine plus pour-over wills naming the trust as residual beneficiary. Fund the trust by retitling investment accounts, real estate, and (post-recap) the voting business interest. Avoids approximately $100,000 of GA probate cost on a ~$5M probate estate at midrange 2% attorney fees (range $50,000–$150,000), and — more importantly — avoids the public probate process entirely. Update beneficiary designations on retirement accounts and life insurance to align with the new structure.

bullets.bold_imperative: Execute the full core document set: POAs, healthcare directives, HIPAA, guardianship.
bullets.briefing: Durable financial POAs and healthcare directives for Marcus and Catherine. HIPAA authorizations. Guardianship designation for Sophie (and James while still a minor) — the 2014 wills do not name one. Adult-child HIPAA and healthcare POA for Olivia (19, at UGA) — without these you cannot legally receive medical information or make decisions for her in an emergency. Direct attorney cost: $8,000–$18,000; conservatorship/emergency court order avoidance value alone is $5,000–$25,000, before the qualitative value of having the documents when needed.

bullets.bold_imperative: Run a complete beneficiary-designation audit.
bullets.briefing: PSA pulls every retirement account, life insurance policy, and TOD/POD designation; reconciles them against the new estate plan; surfaces unintended beneficiaries (common after 12 years); preserves stretch-IRA optionality for non-spouse heirs; and documents the updated set in PSA's file and with each custodian. Avoids probate exposure of 1–3% of asset value on assets that should pass by designation rather than will.

subsections.heading: 12B. Annual Exclusion Gifting + Children's Trusts
subsections.intro: Annual exclusion gifting is the simplest, most reliable estate-removal tool available; the children's trusts are the destination vessel that enforces the age-35 rule and prepares the receiving structure for the much larger downstream GRAT and IDGT transfers.

bullets.bold_imperative: Begin a $152K/year annual exclusion gifting program in January.
bullets.briefing: 4 donees (Olivia, James, Sophie, plus a 4th donee added through Crummey-noticed irrevocable trust mechanics) × 2 spouses × $19K = $152K per year. Over a 20-year horizon, this removes $3.04M from the taxable estate, producing $1,216,000 of estate-tax savings at the 40% federal rate (range $1.2M–$2.6M including compound growth on gifted assets). CPA files Form 709 annually with gift-splitting election; Estate Attorney runs Crummey notice mechanics for any trust gifts.

bullets.bold_imperative: Establish three children's trusts (one per child) with a corporate trustee.
bullets.briefing: Estate Attorney drafts three separate trusts — Olivia, James, Sophie — with HEMS distribution standards, age-35 enforcement matching the family rule, and GST-exempt drafting (see 12E). Corporate trustee preferred for institutional independence; this matters most when the children are adults questioning trustee decisions. Establish Crummey notice procedures up front so annual-exclusion mechanics work cleanly. Cumulative estate-tax savings across the trusts (annual exclusion + GRAT remainder + IDGT distributions + compound growth) at 40% federal rate: approximately $6,000,000 at the midpoint, range $4M–$8M.

subsections.heading: 12C. ILIT + Survivorship UL — Estate Liquidity
subsections.intro: Estate tax is due in cash within nine months of death. A business worth $30M+ that is illiquid for 12–18 months produces a forced-sale problem unless the liquidity is pre-funded. The ILIT-owned SUL solves it.

bullets.bold_imperative: Form the ILIT before binding any new life insurance.
bullets.briefing: Estate Attorney drafts the ILIT with Catherine and an independent trustee structure; the ILIT must own the SUL from inception so the death proceeds are outside both estates and avoid the §2042 incidents-of-ownership problem. Pair with annual Crummey-noticed premium funding workflow. Existing $2M term on Marcus is not in trust; we evaluate whether to transfer it (3-year lookback applies) or replace.

bullets.bold_imperative: Bind a $10M survivorship UL on Marcus and Catherine, ILIT-owned from day one.
bullets.briefing: Recommended face $10M (range $8M–$12M) sized to projected federal estate tax at second death plus a cushion to leave ILIT remainder for grandchildren. Insurance Broker completes underwriting on both lives; bind once underwriting clears. Keeps approximately $3.6M+ of estate tax (40% of face) outside the taxable estate. Premium quote-driven; we will model affordability against the cash-flow runway from PTET savings (Section 3) and the tiered cash structures in Sections 2 and 8.

subsections.heading: 12D. GRAT, SLAT, IDGT — The Wealth-Transfer Layers
subsections.intro: These three vehicles are the heart of the estate-tax mitigation strategy. Each requires the entity work in Section 1 to be complete first — specifically the F-reorg and the voting/non-voting recapitalization that produces the discounted non-voting interest used to fund them.

bullets.bold_imperative: Fund a 3-Year Zeroed-Out GRAT with ~$8.2M of non-voting Holdco units.
bullets.briefing: Estate Attorney drafts the GRAT; CPA runs the Walton-zeroed annuity calculation at the current §7520 rate (~5.0%, May 2026); Valuation Provider issues the qualified appraisal at funding. Any growth above the §7520 hurdle passes to the children's trusts free of gift tax. At an 18% growth scenario over the 3-year term, value transferred is approximately $4.2M (range $0–$6.5M; $0 if business stalls, higher if a transaction occurs during or after the term — which is increasingly plausible given the inbound interest). Strategy is asymmetric in your favor: downside is cost of attorney/appraisal; upside is the entire growth above the hurdle.

bullets.bold_imperative: Fund a SLAT for Catherine and the children with $5M of appreciating assets.
bullets.briefing: Estate Attorney drafts the SLAT with Catherine and the children as beneficiaries, HEMS distribution standard, independent trustee, §2036-safe drafting (Marcus is grantor; he cannot be a beneficiary). Funding asset selection optimized for appreciation potential (real estate, business interest, or securities). At 7% growth over 25 years, $5M grows to approximately $27M outside both estates — approximately $10.8M of estate tax avoided at 40% (range $5.4M–$16.2M across growth-rate and horizon sensitivity). CPA files Form 709 documenting lifetime exemption use.

bullets.bold_imperative: Execute an IDGT installment sale of non-voting units in Year 2.
bullets.briefing: After the recap is settled and the GRAT and SLAT are funded, Estate Attorney drafts the IDGT with §675(4) substitute power for grantor-trust status. Seed the IDGT with ~10% of intended sale value via lifetime-exemption gift (~$1.75M). Sell the non-voting interest to the IDGT at appraised value, take back a 9-year AFR-rate installment note. Grantor-trust status lets Marcus pay the income tax on the trust's income — a tax-free gift to the trust's beneficiaries, often called the 'tax burn.' CPA prepares the §1014 basis tracking memo and Rev. Rul. 85-13 confirmation. Estimated additional value transferred outside the estate: $10M at midpoint, range $6M–$15M depending on growth and timing of any transaction relative to the note term.

subsections.heading: 12E. GST / Dynasty Layer
subsections.intro: Once the wealth is transferred outside the estate, the question is how long it stays outside. GST exemption allocation determines whether the assets ride free of transfer tax for one generation, two, or — with the right trust situs — indefinitely.

bullets.bold_imperative: Decide trust situs: Georgia vs. Delaware/South Dakota/Nevada.
bullets.briefing: Georgia has a Rule Against Perpetuities that limits dynasty-trust duration. Delaware, South Dakota, and Nevada permit perpetual dynasty trusts. For the GST-allocated structures (children's trusts and IDGT), the situs decision determines whether the assets compound free of transfer tax for ~90 years (GA) or indefinitely (DE/SD/NV). This is one of the State C-style decisions that surfaces in Decisions Needed; default recommendation is a perpetual-situs jurisdiction for the GST-allocated trusts given the multi-generational horizon and the magnitude of the wealth at stake.

bullets.bold_imperative: Allocate GST exemption explicitly at funding and track inclusion ratios annually.
bullets.briefing: CPA files Form 709 with explicit GST exemption allocation at each funding event (children's trusts, GRAT remainder, IDGT seed, SLAT). Maintains annual GST exemption allocation tracking and inclusion-ratio documentation — without this, future distributions from the trust face a 40% GST tax that this entire structure was designed to avoid. On $30M of allocated dynasty trust corpus growing at 5% over 60 years, future GST tax avoided is approximately $224M at the midpoint (range $50M–$500M depending on growth rates and horizon).

closer_paragraph.label: Combined estate impact
closer_paragraph.body: Adding the layers: ~$100K of probate avoidance, ~$1.2M from annual exclusion gifting (low-end, no growth), ~$6M from the children's trusts cumulative housing, ~$4.2M from the GRAT remainder, ~$10.8M from the SLAT, ~$10M from the IDGT installment sale, ~$3.6M of estate tax avoided through ILIT-owned SUL — and on the multi-generational horizon, GST exemption allocation potentially preserves an order-of-magnitude larger amount across grandchildren and great-grandchildren. Done in the right sequence, with the entity work in Section 1 completed first, the projected $11.4M–$17.6M estate-tax exposure is not just neutralized — it is converted into a multi-generational dynasty structure. The professional fees over 24 months are a small fraction of any one of those layers.
