coverage_table.notes: 60% income replacement; 24-month own-occ then any-occ. Inadequate for HNW owner. No individual LTD. Catherine has no DI coverage. No business overhead expense coverage.

$5M limit. Other business coverages: GL $2M occurrence/$4M aggregate, Workers Comp in place, commercial auto fleet in place, Cyber $1M (likely under-insured given customer mix). No buy/sell life funding. No key person coverage on Derek or controller.

goals_priorities.intro_paragraph: These are the goals we identified together during discovery. Every recommendation in this plan ties back to one or more of them. We will revisit and refine these at every annual review.

goals.what_this_means_in_practice: Ranked: (1) Maximize after-tax sale proceeds — position HIS for 3-5 year transaction window with structural and tax planning that compounds value retained. (2) Take care of Derek — treat his 12% fairly through any transition, both economically and as key continuity person. (3) Estate planning — reduce projected $11M+ estate-tax exposure via layered structure of trusts, gifts, and life insurance. (4) Entity & real estate structure — separate Kennesaw real estate from operating company; build holding-company layer for future flexibility. (5) Tax strategy — Georgia PTET election, optimize W-2 vs K-1 mix, evaluate cost segregation, §469 grouping, cash-balance plan. (6) Children's provision without demotivation — fund education and meaningful head start with controls preventing unrestricted access before age 35. (7) Catherine's real estate vision — build structural capacity for post-exit real estate development deployment. (8) Risk management — match life, disability, key-person coverage to actual exposures. (9) Personal investment diversification — build wealth outside the business; systematic taxable brokerage funding; structured asset location. (10) Philanthropic vehicles — DAF near-term, family foundation post-liquidity with children eventually involved in governance.

Near-term DAF; family foundation post-liquidity with children involved in governance as values-formation mechanism (Catherine's hard preference for foundation, not just DAF). Specific causes: (1) children's hospital where Catherine serves on the board; (2) trade scholarships at North Cobb Tech (Marcus's alma mater).

Children provided for but with restrictions — no unrestricted access to inherited wealth before age 35 (hard rule). Catherine wants children involved in foundation governance. Marcus and Catherine expect to provide financial support to Marcus's mother (Eleanor, 78) within 5-10 years. Family wealth communication has been minimal: Olivia generally aware; James and Sophie not yet briefed. Catherine wants this to change deliberately as children enter adulthood but is cautious about timing.

Open transaction type (likely strategic for valuation) but requires buyer that retains operations and Derek's role. Stock sale preferred; willingness to discuss F-reorg/asset structures if material price impact. Open to 1-3 years transition role post-sale; not interested in long-term employment. Hard constraints: will not sell to a buyer who would relocate operations or eliminate Derek's role; no strip-and-flip economics — intact business non-negotiable. Marcus will not personally guarantee debt for any new venture post-exit.

Marcus values the dignity of skilled trades work — built HIS deliberately as a place where field people are paid and respected like the professionals they are. Wants to extend that through trade scholarships at North Cobb Tech. Catherine values community health access (her board work at the children's hospital) and sees philanthropy as how the children learn responsibility for wealth they did not create. Both deeply value the operating partner relationship with Derek; "taking care of Derek" is a values position, not just a planning priority.

glossary.intro_paragraph: Plain-English definitions for the technical terms used in this plan.

entries.plain_english_definition: Generally disallows passive losses (like rental real estate) from offsetting active business income — but a grouping election can change that.
Disability insurance that reimburses the business for fixed operating costs while the owner is disabled.
Life insurance owned by the business on key employees, often used to informally fund deferred compensation arrangements.
A charitable account where you take the deduction at funding but recommend grants to charities over time. The simplest charitable vehicle.
Replaces a portion of income (typically 60–70%) if you cannot work due to illness or injury.
A trust that pays you back the full value of what you contribute (plus an IRS-set interest rate) over a set term. Any growth above that rate passes to your heirs free of gift tax.

A trust structured so the grantor pays the income tax on the trust's earnings (an additional gift-tax-free transfer) but the assets are outside the grantor's estate. "Defective" is a term of art — it works exactly as intended.
A trust that owns a life insurance policy so that proceeds pass outside the insured's taxable estate.
Non-binding outline of deal terms, typically signed before due diligence in a transaction.
An election that pays state tax at the entity level rather than the individual level — bypassing the $10,000 SALT cap on personal returns.
A detailed analysis of a business's earnings done by an independent firm, typically commissioned ahead of a transaction.
A non-qualified retirement plan funded by the employer for select key employees, typically with vesting schedules to encourage retention.

This document contains tax-related considerations. Nothing in this document should be construed as tax or legal advice. Always consult with your own tax or legal advisor concerning your specific situation.

Estimates and projections shown in this plan reflect assumptions about future tax law, valuations, growth rates, and your circumstances. Actual results may vary materially. Specifically: (i) business valuation ranges are preliminary and not a formal valuation opinion; (ii) tax savings estimates are based on current law as of the plan date and your projected income; (iii) GRAT and IDGT projected wealth transfers depend on actual business performance versus the IRS-set hurdle rate during the trust term, and adverse performance can reduce or eliminate the planning benefit; (iv) insurance premium estimates are based on standard underwriting and may differ following actual underwriting.
This plan is a living document and will be updated based on the cadence outlined in Meeting Cadence & Next Steps.
